chunk_summary: This section discusses gastrostomy decision-making and peri-procedure risk mitigation in neurodisability, emphasizing shared decision-making, pre-operative optimization, and management of complications to improve feeding outcomes and reduce caregiver burden.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of gastrostomy in managing feeding challenges in neurodisability.
Learn pre-operative optimization strategies to mitigate peri-procedure risks.
Recognize common complications and their management post-gastrostomy placement.
Explore evidence-based practices for early feeding and dislodgement planning.
key_facts: Gastrostomy reduces feeding time and caregiver distress, with studies showing improved growth in severe cerebral palsy cases.
Pre-operative optimization includes correcting anemia, malnutrition, and constipation, and reviewing respiratory stability and seizure control.
Antibiotic prophylaxis reduces peristomal infection risk, and early feeding within 3 hours is safe in stable children using iso-osmolar feeds.
Dislodgement protocols treat the tract as immature for 6-12 weeks, requiring surgical input for early replacement.
High-risk anatomy (e.g., severe scoliosis, ventriculoperitoneal shunt) may necessitate laparoscopic-assisted placement.
reference_quote: Studies in severe CP show improved growth and reduced caregiver feeding time after gastrostomy, but counseling should also cover common minor complications (granulation tissue, leakage, superficial infection).
document_key_topics: Evidence-based feeding protocols and dislodgement planning
